summary: The plan is directionally strong and covers the main data flow, but it has two significant reliability/performance gaps that should be resolved before implementation.

issues.title: Locale detection in both layouts risks route-wide dynamic rendering regression
issues.description: The plan resolves anonymous preference from `Accept-Language` and calls `resolveUnitPreference()` in both `(app)` and `(main)` layouts. In Next.js App Router, reading request headers in a layout makes that subtree request-dynamic, which can disable static optimization/full-route caching for all pages under that group. Applying this to `(main)` can create a significant performance regression.
issues.suggestion: Constrain header-based detection to routes that are already dynamic, or move anonymous locale inference to a client-side initialization path (or equivalent non-layout strategy) for `(main)`. Explicitly verify build output/caching behavior before rollout.

issues.title: Preference mutation path is underspecified for failure handling
issues.description: Step 3 says the provider has an optimistic setter that calls the server action, while Step 4 also says `UnitSetting` uses the server action. The plan does not define a single authoritative mutation path or rollback/error behavior if the action fails. This can leave UI state inconsistent with persisted DB state.
issues.suggestion: Define one write path (preferably `useUnitPreference().setPreference`) that handles pending state, rollback on failure, and user-visible error feedback. Keep `UnitSetting` as a thin consumer of that API.
